planting.note: Root cuttings in warm conditions; a reliable young bloomer.

directions: Root a cutting of the small round fuzzy leaves and grow this bushy, easy species in bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and give it good airflow to keep the soft foliage healthy. It blooms readily with clusters of small white flowers marked red at the center, scented sweetly in the evening.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season.

directions: Root a cutting of the modest, softly haired leaves and grow it in bright indirect light with a chunky, well-drained mix. Water when the surface dries and keep it warm and reasonably humid. It produces striking clusters of velvety deep-red star flowers with a paler central corona, a favorite for the color.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm active growth; a prolific young bloomer.

directions: Root a cutting of the small narrow leaves and grow this compact, exceptionally free-flowering species in bright indirect light. Keep watering even but let the surface dry slightly between. It blooms almost continually in warm weather, producing tidy clusters of bright pink flowers with a yellow-orange center.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm season; small-leaved and quick to flower.

directions: Root a cutting of the tiny leaves and grow this dainty trailer in a small hanging pot with bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and provide gentle humidity. Despite its small size it flowers freely, bearing clusters of little star-shaped flowers in shades of pink to red with a light fragrance.

planting.note: Root in spring and keep cool; a Himalayan highland species.

directions: Root a cutting of the small pointed leaves and grow this cool-loving highland species with more humidity and less heat than typical hoyas. Keep the mix lightly moist but airy and shelter it from direct sun. It produces neat clusters of white star flowers with pink centers and is treasured as a compact basket plant.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm humid conditions.

directions: Root a cutting of the large, thick leaves marked with deeply impressed veins, often edged in cream on variegated forms. Give bright indirect light, a support to climb, and a coarse mix kept lightly moist. It grows into a bold foliage plant and eventually bears rounded clusters of small pinkish-white flowers.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm, dry-leaning conditions.

directions: Root a cutting of the very thick, rigid succulent leaves in a gritty mix and water sparingly, treating this much like a succulent. It tolerates bright, even direct light and short dry spells, so err toward under-watering. Compact clusters of waxy white flowers with pink-red centers form on short stalks and are heavily fragrant at night.

planting.note: Root cuttings in the warm growing season.

directions: Root a cutting of the broad leathery leaves and train this Philippine climber up a support in warm, bright, humid conditions. Water when the top inch dries and feed during active growth. It produces large, full clusters of deep pink to red star flowers with a reflexed corona, making a bold display when mature.

planting.note: Root in spring and keep cooler; a montane species.

directions: Root a cutting of the large, hairy, prominently veined leaves and grow this Himalayan foothill species somewhat cooler and humid. Keep the coarse mix evenly moist but never waterlogged and give bright indirect light. It bears dense globe-shaped clusters of small creamy-white flowers on established plants.

planting.note: Root in spring and keep cool; a highland species.

directions: Root a cutting of the softly haired leaves and grow this cool-growing Himalayan species with good humidity and protection from hot sun. Keep the mix lightly moist and airy, avoiding both drought and sogginess. It produces pendant clusters of white, fuzzy, sweetly scented flowers with a pink to red center.

planting.note: Root cuttings in warm active growth.

directions: Root a cutting of the large, thick, veined leaves and train this robust climber up a support in bright indirect light. Water when the top inch dries and give warmth and moderate humidity. It grows quickly for a large-leaved hoya and bears full rounded clusters of white to pale pink flowers with red centers.

planting.note: Grown as a warm indoor pot plant, so sow or pot up any time in the warm season under bright indirect light.

directions: Surface-sow the dust-fine seed on a moist peat-based mix and do not cover it, keeping the surface humid until the seedlings appear. Grow in bright light out of direct midday sun and water when the top of the mix begins to dry, avoiding wet crowns. Large lavender-blue flowers with pale throats open on branching stalks through spring and summer once the rosette matures.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in warmth in spring so the single leaf has a full season to develop.

directions: Sow on the surface of a gritty, free-draining mix and keep warm and lightly moist, never soggy. This unifoliate species forms one very large felted leaf that eventually throws dense spikes of brick-red to dull pink flowers. Reduce water as the plant slows after flowering, since it tends to be short-lived once it has bloomed heavily.

planting.note: Sow or divide in the warm months and grow on as a frost-free pot plant.

directions: Scatter the fine seed on damp mix and leave uncovered under bright, filtered light. Keep the rosette evenly moist during growth and give it a cool, drier rest in winter. Nodding pale-blue to white flowers marked with darker throat lines appear in autumn on wiry stalks above the soft green leaves.

planting.note: Sow in spring warmth to give the giant solitary leaf a long growing season.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and humid until germination, then pot on into a rich but open mix. The plant produces a single enormous leaf, dark green above and purple beneath, that can reach two to three feet before flowering. Tall branched panicles of violet-blue flowers rise above the leaf, after which this monocarpic species declines and should be resown.

planting.note: Best sown in spring so plants flower freely by late summer.

directions: Sow the fine seed uncovered on a moist surface and maintain humidity until seedlings establish. Grow in bright shade and keep lightly moist while in active growth. As the name suggests it carries many small pale-blue to mauve flowers with contrasting throats over a long summer season.

planting.note: Raise from seed indoors or pot up divisions in the warm season.

directions: Press the tiny seed onto damp mix without covering and keep humid and warm until it sprouts. This species contributed much of the pink and magenta color to modern hybrids, so expect rose to mauve flowers with yellow-and-red throat markings. Water moderately and protect from cold drafts and hot direct sun.

planting.note: Sow in spring so the single large leaf can mature over one long season.

directions: Surface-sow onto moist mix and cover the pot to hold humidity until germination. The plant makes one very large puckered leaf and then a dense conical spike of small pale-violet flowers. It is monocarpic, so let seed set if you want to keep the species going, then start fresh plants.

planting.note: Sow in warmth in spring; this unifoliate species needs a full season before it flowers.

directions: Sow uncovered on a damp free-draining mix and keep humid and warm until seedlings appear. It forms one broad leaf and later sends up long-tubed white flowers that are notably fragrant, especially in the evening. Grow it on steadily, then allow it to seed since it dies after flowering.

planting.note: Take leaf cuttings or divide in spring and grow on frost-free indoors.

directions: Propagate by pushing a section of leaf cut across its midrib into damp mix, or by dividing an older clump. This classic free-flowering hybrid carries pale blue flowers veined with darker lines almost year-round in good light. Keep the mix lightly moist, feed occasionally with a dilute balanced fertilizer, and remove spent stalks to encourage more.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in spring or summer and grow as a warm houseplant.

directions: Increase by leaf-section cuttings set upright in a light, moist mix under cover. This award-winning selection bears crisp white flowers etched with fine blue-violet veining over a compact rosette. Site it in bright indirect light, water when the surface dries, and it will bloom for many months.

planting.note: Propagate from leaf cuttings in the warm months and keep frost-free.

directions: Root leaf cuttings or divide established plants in spring. This vigorous, award-winning cultivar produces blue upper petals over a yellow-marked lower lip on freely branching stems. Give bright filtered light, avoid soggy soil, and deadhead regularly for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Take leaf cuttings in spring or summer and grow on indoors.

directions: Multiply by leaf-section cuttings inserted into a moist, airy mix under a humidity cover. Prized for masses of small clear-blue flowers held on slender stems above the foliage, it flowers very generously. Keep it in bright light without harsh sun, water moderately, and feed lightly during the growing season.

planting.note: Propagate from leaf cuttings in the warm season and keep frost-free.

directions: Raise new plants from cross-cut leaf sections rooted in a warm, humid propagator. The white flowers of this cultivar are speckled and netted with purple, giving a distinctive dotted look. Grow in bright indirect light, let the surface dry slightly between waterings, and remove old flower stalks to prompt fresh spikes.

planting.note: Take leaf cuttings in spring and grow as a warm indoor plant.

directions: Propagate by inserting leaf-blade sections cut across the midrib into damp cutting mix. This selection is valued for its rich deep-red flowers, an unusual color among Cape primroses, carried well above the leaves. Keep it warm and evenly moist in bright shade and feed lightly to sustain repeat flushes.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in the warm months and keep indoors year-round.

directions: Increase by leaf cuttings set upright in a light, moisture-retentive mix under cover. This compact, award-winning cultivar bears deep violet-purple flowers with a darker, near-black throat over a neat rosette. Provide bright filtered light, water when the top of the mix dries, and deadhead to keep it blooming for months.

planting.note: Propagate from leaf cuttings in spring or summer, kept frost-free.

directions: Root cross-cut leaf sections in a warm, humid propagator to make new plants. This popular cultivar produces soft pink flowers with a paler netted throat over a long flowering season. Grow in bright indirect light, keep lightly moist, and feed with a dilute high-potash food to encourage bloom.

planting.note: Take leaf cuttings in the warm season and grow on indoors.

directions: Propagate from leaf-section cuttings inserted into a moist, well-aerated mix under a clear cover. This graceful cultivar carries white flowers with a soft yellow flush in the throat above fresh green foliage. Keep it in bright shade, water moderately, and remove finished stems to prolong the display.

planting.note: Root stem-tip cuttings in spring; move outdoor baskets out only after frost has passed.

directions: Unlike rosette types, root short stem-tip cuttings which strike easily in moist mix. This branching, semi-trailing plant suits hanging baskets and pours out small lavender-blue flowers all summer. Pinch the stems to keep it bushy, water when the surface dries, and shelter it from frost and hot midday sun.

planting.note: Root leaf or stem cuttings in the warm season and grow on indoors year-round.

directions: Propagate from leaf cuttings or by pinning down the trailing stems until they root. This species has a spreading, semi-trailing habit and is the parent of most modern trailing African violets, bearing pale blue-violet flowers. Grow in bright indirect light, keep the mix lightly moist with room-temperature water, and avoid wetting the leaves in strong light.

planting.note: Start leaf cuttings in warmth and grow the small rosette on indoors.

directions: Increase by leaf cuttings set into a small pot of light, moist mix under humid conditions. This dwarf species forms a tiny rosette and produces pale lilac-white flowers, and it has been used to breed miniature hybrids. Keep it warm in bright filtered light and water carefully so the small crown never sits wet.

planting.note: Root leaf cuttings in warmth and grow on as a frost-free houseplant.

directions: Propagate from healthy leaves inserted at an angle into a light, moist rooting mix. This species has velvety toothed leaves, often red-backed, and small deep violet flowers with a paler eye. Grow in bright indirect light, water from below or take care to keep foliage dry, and maintain warm, humid conditions.

planting.note: Take leaf cuttings in the warm months and grow indoors year-round.

directions: Root leaf cuttings in a small pot of moist, open mix kept warm and humid. This species forms a flattish rosette of green leaves and bears blue-violet flowers with a small yellow eye. Give it bright filtered light, water with tepid water, and keep it evenly moist but never waterlogged.

planting.note: Root a leaf cutting in the warm season and grow the plantlets on indoors.

directions: Snap off a healthy leaf with a short stalk and root it in moist mix, potting up the young plantlets that form at the base. This early, historically important cultivar bears simple single blue-violet flowers over dark green rounded leaves. Grow in bright indirect light, water from below with room-temperature water, and feed lightly to keep it in near-constant bloom.

planting.note: Root a leaf cutting in warmth and grow the small rosette on indoors.

directions: Propagate from a leaf cutting set in a small pot of light, moist mix under humid conditions. This popular miniature stays compact and produces semidouble deep-blue flowers with a fringed edge over a tidy rosette. Keep it in bright indirect light, water carefully to protect the small crown, and pot in a shallow container sized to the rosette.

planting.note: Grown indoors year-round; pot up rooted leaf plantlets any time in a warm room.

directions: Root a healthy leaf with an inch of stalk in a light peat mix, keeping the crown at soil level so it never rots. Give bright indirect light and water from below with tepid water to avoid spotting the fuzzy leaves. Feed a dilute balanced fertilizer and remove spent blooms, and clusters of flowers will appear on and off through the year.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors under bright light in late winter or early spring.

directions: Scatter the dust-fine seed on the surface of a moist, sterile mix and do not cover it, as light aids germination. Move seedlings to individual pots once they can be handled and grow them cool and bright out of direct sun. Water only when the top of the soil dries, and long stems of trumpet flowers will rise above the strappy leaves.

planting.note: Set rooted stem cuttings into hanging pots in spring for a trailing summer display.

directions: Take short tip cuttings and root them in a gritty, free-draining mix, since this species dislikes soggy roots. Grow it in bright indirect light in a hanging container so the wiry stems and small fuzzy leaves can cascade. Let the mix approach dryness between waterings, and pale lavender flowers on thin stalks will keep coming through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant rooted stolon plantlets in spring into a hanging basket where the runners can dangle.

directions: Root the small plantlets carried on trailing runners in a light, moisture-retentive mix and plant them at their original depth. Provide warmth, bright indirect light, and steady humidity for the low mat of hairy green leaves. Keep the soil lightly moist, and fringed white flowers with a purple-speckled throat will open along the runners.

planting.note: Start the scaly rhizomes in spring, about an inch deep in a warm, humid spot.

directions: Lay the caterpillar-like rhizomes horizontally about an inch below the surface of a rich, well-drained mix and keep them warm. Grow in bright indirect light with good humidity, feeding lightly as the velvety, dark-veined leaves develop. Spikes of nodding bell flowers in orange and yellow follow, after which let the plant dry off and rest the dormant rhizomes.

planting.note: Plant the scaly rhizomes an inch deep after the soil warms, or grow in large pots.

directions: Set the rhizomes about an inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil in a warm, sheltered position or a roomy container. Give consistent moisture and part shade through the growing season as the tall stems and mint-scented foliage build. Fragrant lavender-purple bells with a mealy white eye open in late summer, after which the plant dies back to overwinter as a dormant rhizome.

planting.note: Start the small rhizomes in spring for a burst of tubular flowers in fall and winter.

directions: Plant the slender scaly rhizomes just below the surface in a light, well-drained mix and keep them warm and evenly moist. Grow in bright indirect light, and the wandering rhizomes will produce upright leafy stems over the season. Slim, glowing orange trumpets appear as days shorten in autumn, then reduce watering to let the plant rest.

planting.note: Pot the tuber with its top at soil level once temperatures stay reliably warm.

directions: Nestle the rounded tuber into a well-drained mix with its crown just at the surface and water sparingly until growth resumes. Give warmth and bright indirect light, increasing water as the soft, downy leaves expand. Hooded bright red tubular flowers rise on short stems, and after bloom let the soil dry so the tuber can rest.

planting.note: Grow the large woody caudex partly exposed in a gritty mix and start watering as buds break in spring.

directions: Plant the swollen caudex in a very free-draining, gritty mix with its top sitting above the soil like a small boulder. Begin watering only when the silvery, densely woolly new leaves emerge in spring, and keep it warm with bright light. Salmon-pink tubular flowers open among the felted foliage, then withhold water through the long dormancy to keep the caudex firm.

planting.note: Surface-sow the tiny seed in a covered container to hold humidity; it often self-sows in a terrarium.

directions: Scatter the near-microscopic seed on the surface of a moist mix inside a covered dish or terrarium and do not bury it. Keep it warm, humid, and softly lit, and the plants remain thumbnail-sized as they establish a tiny tuber. Pale lavender slipper flowers appear almost continuously, and the plant frequently seeds itself around a closed case.

planting.note: Plant the spreading tubers a couple of inches deep around the last frost date in a warm border.

directions: Set the small tubers two to three inches deep in well-drained soil where they can slowly colonize by underground stems. Give plenty of sun and moderate summer moisture as the upright, softly hairy stems grow. Long, pure white tubular flowers open in late summer with a powerful evening fragrance; in colder zones mulch heavily or lift the tubers to store dry.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring and plant into a hanging pot with epiphytic, airy mix.

directions: Root four-inch stem cuttings in a loose, bark-based mix and plant several to a hanging container for fullness. Grow warm in bright indirect light, letting the top of the mix dry slightly between waterings. The plant is prized for its long trailing stems of dark leaves mottled pale green beneath, accented by small maroon-and-green tubular flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into hanging baskets in an airy, well-drained mix.

directions: Insert tip cuttings into a bark-and-peat mix and pot several together so the arching stems fill the basket. Keep the plant warm and brightly but indirectly lit, watering when the surface begins to dry. Clusters of orange to flame-red tubular flowers form at the stem tips, so avoid disturbing developing buds and provide good humidity to hold them.

planting.note: Start the tuber an inch deep in spring in a warm, humid spot.

directions: Plant the tuber about an inch deep in a rich, well-drained mix and keep it warm and evenly moist as growth begins. Grow in bright indirect light, and the glossy, coppery-veined leaves will form a lush mound. Papery orange calyces hold small golden flowers for a long show, after which let the plant dry down and store the tuber over its rest period.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into hanging baskets so the long stems can cascade.

directions: Root tip cuttings in a light, free-draining mix and group several in a hanging pot to build a curtain of tiny round leaves. Keep it warm and humid in bright indirect light, watering when the surface just dries. Hooded scarlet flowers with a yellow throat stud the trailing stems, most heavily after a slightly cooler, drier spell that sets the buds.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed indoors, or root a leaf, in a warm humid setting.

directions: Sow the tiny seed on the surface of a moist mix and keep it covered and warm, as it germinates readily and can self-sow. Grow the compact rosette of fleshy leaves in bright indirect light and water when the top of the soil dries. Slender stalks carry white flowers marked with violet in the throat over a long season, and leaves root easily to make more plants.

planting.note: Root a leaf with its stalk in spring; growth and bloom are slow and seasonal.

directions: Root a whole leaf with a short stalk in a light mix and be patient, as this plant grows slowly into a flat, symmetrical rosette. Keep it cool to warm in bright indirect light and water carefully to avoid wetting the crown of overlapping hairy leaves. Short-stemmed white to violet flowers open low over the rosette, typically in a single seasonal flush.

planting.note: Surface-sow the fine seed in spring and grow on slowly; plants take a few years to flower.

directions: Sow the dust-like seed on the surface in early spring and leave it uncovered, since light triggers germination and seedlings develop slowly. Plant established rosettes sideways into a cool, north-facing rock crevice or wall so water drains from the crown. The wrinkled, hairy rosettes send up flat, potato-flower-like blooms of lilac-blue with a golden eye in late spring.

planting.note: Divide or plant rosettes in spring into a shaded crevice with sharp drainage.

directions: Tuck divided rosettes into a shaded, gritty crevice or the vertical face of a wall so the crown never sits wet. This desiccation-tolerant alpine can shrivel in drought and revive after rain, so give it a cool, humus-rich pocket. Sprays of pale lilac tubular flowers rise above the leathery, toothed rosettes in late spring.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or bulbil-grown offsets in spring in a shaded, sheltered spot.

directions: Set the running rhizomes just below the surface of a moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in shade or dappled light. Keep it sheltered from cold winds and evenly watered through the growing season as leafy stems rise. Spikes of yellow foxglove-like flowers with a red-flushed throat appear in late summer; mulch the crowns for winter in colder gardens.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in spring into a hanging basket for long cascading stems.

directions: Root tip cuttings in a light, free-draining mix and plant several in a hanging container so the paired glossy leaves trail in long ropes. Grow warm in bright indirect light, letting the surface dry slightly between waterings and keeping humidity up. Large hooded scarlet flowers appear along the stems, most freely after a slightly cooler, drier winter rest that initiates buds.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Pot rooted pups just deep enough to hold them upright in a fast-draining bark or orchid mix. Keep the central cup topped with rainwater and let the outer medium dry slightly between waterings. A yellow-and-red flower spike rises from a maturing rosette, after which the plant sends up new offsets to replace it.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this species tolerates brief cool spells better than most.

directions: Give this stout, spiny rosette a coarse potting mix and a container heavy enough to counter its top growth. It handles brighter light and a touch more cold than tender relatives, though the leaf tips still scorch below freezing. Expect a rose-pink bract cluster topped with violet blooms once the plant reaches full size.

planting.note: Establish offsets in spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Root pups in a loose epiphytic blend and keep them warm and humid. The soft green leaves often flush coppery red in bright shade, signaling the plant is ready to bloom. A branched red-and-lavender inflorescence emerges from the cup and lasts for weeks.

planting.note: Pot up offsets in spring under bright, filtered light for best banding.

directions: Grown mainly for its cream leaves cross-banded in maroon, this compact rosette colors best in bright indirect light. Use a very open mix and avoid letting the base sit wet. A short spike of red bracts and pale flowers appears at maturity, followed by a ring of pups.

planting.note: Set out divisions in spring in warm, humid conditions.

directions: This classic hybrid is prized for its glossy, lacquer-red wine-colored foliage that shines in bright filtered light. Plant offsets shallowly in a fast-draining mix and keep the cup filled with clean water. A pendant spike of coral and blue flowers hangs from the mature rosette.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; brighter sun deepens the leaf color.

directions: A tough, spiny rosette that blushes bronze-red under strong light and tolerates more sun than most Aechmeas. Use a gritty, well-drained mix and water when the medium approaches dry. The dense cone of golden-yellow flowers on a red-woolly stalk is unmistakable at bloom time.

planting.note: Establish pups in spring in a large, stable container.

directions: This vigorous clumping species forms broad green rosettes that eventually crowd a container. Provide bright indirect light and a coarse, open medium, keeping the cups topped up. A tall branched spike of golden flowers rises well above the leaves in summer.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring; strong light brings out the leaf blush.

directions: A compact, sun-tolerant hybrid whose banded leaves turn rosy in bright light. Grow it lean in a gritty mix and water sparingly, letting the medium dry between drinks. It offsets freely and produces a short spike of red bracts with yellow flowers.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in a shaded, humid location.

directions: This slender green rosette prefers filtered light and steady humidity, whether potted or mounted. Keep the vase filled with soft water and mist the foliage in dry air. The upright spike shifts from green striped with brown to bright red at the top as flowering begins.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in bright shade with high humidity.

directions: A parent of many commercial Guzmania hybrids, it forms a smooth green rosette that resents direct sun. Grow it in an airy, peat-and-bark mix kept lightly moist and top the cup with clean water. The long-lasting inflorescence carries slender scarlet or pink bracts.

planting.note: Pot up offsets in spring in bright indirect light.

directions: This tidy florist hybrid is grown for its yellow to soft-orange bract head above glossy green leaves. Keep it out of hot sun, water the cup with distilled or rainwater, and let the mix stay barely moist. After the long-lived bracts fade, remove the spent rosette and grow on the pups.

planting.note: Establish offsets in spring under filtered light.

directions: Valued for its deep wine-red bract cluster, this hybrid holds color for many weeks indoors. Grow in bright shade in a loose, quick-draining medium and keep the central cup filled with soft water. Once bloom fades, the mother plant is replaced by its offsets.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in warm, bright-shade conditions.

directions: A popular gift-plant hybrid whose bright orange-red bracts sit atop a rosette of shiny strap leaves. Shield it from direct sun and keep humidity up, watering into the cup rather than the mix. The colorful head lasts for months before the plant offsets and declines.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in filtered light.

directions: This commercial hybrid produces a tall, vivid red bract spike prized for indoor display. Grow it warm and shaded in an open bark blend, keeping the vase topped with clean water. Divide and replant the pups after the parent rosette finishes flowering.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright filtered light.

directions: Grown for its recurved gray-green leaves dusted silver and flushed purple beneath, this rosette suits bright indirect light. Use a soft, open mix and water into the cup with low-mineral water. A branched, arching spike of yellow flowers on green bracts appears at maturity.

planting.note: Establish offsets in spring in warm, humid shade.

directions: A soft-leaved green rosette that sends up a slender spike of red-and-yellow bracts resembling a parrot's plumage. Grow it in bright shade with steady humidity in a loose, airy medium. Keep the cup filled with rainwater and avoid cold drafts, which spot the leaves.

planting.note: Pot up offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: This large, long-cultivated hybrid throws a tall, branched red flower head well above its smooth green rosette. Give it a roomy pot of open mix, bright indirect light, and clean water in the vase. The dramatic inflorescence holds color for a month or more before the plant offsets.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in bright filtered light.

directions: A flaming-sword-type hybrid grown for both its dark cross-banded leaves and its flattened red flower blade. Provide bright indirect light to keep the banding crisp and water into the cup with soft water. The sword-shaped inflorescence is long-lasting and rises straight from the rosette center.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in a large container with bright light.

directions: This big architectural rosette shows a netted, checkerboard pattern across pale green leaves that intensifies in bright light. Grow it in a heavy pot of coarse mix and keep the generous cup topped with clean water. It is grown chiefly as a foliage specimen, eventually sending up a tall branched flower stalk.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: Prized for pale yellow-green leaves marked with wine-red irregular blotches, this rosette is a foliage favorite. Bright indirect light sharpens the markings without bleaching them; a soft, open mix suits it best. A flattened yellow flower spike eventually rises from the center.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in bright light to develop leaf color.

directions: This spreading hybrid forms open rosettes of glossy leaves that flush red and purple in strong light. Grow it bright but out of scorching midday sun, in a shallow, well-drained mix. Rather than a tall spike, small violet flowers nestle in a colorful, water-filled central cup.

planting.note: Establish offsets in spring under bright, filtered light.

directions: A parent of many modern hybrids, this species reddens through the center of its rosette as blooming nears. Provide bright indirect light and a fast-draining medium, keeping the cup filled with clean water. Blue flowers open low in the flooded center rather than on a raised stalk.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in a large, stable container.

directions: One of the largest Neoregelias, it forms a broad rosette edged with pronounced dark, toothy spines. Give it room, bright light, and a coarse mix, handling the sharp leaves with care. Its center flushes red as blue flowers open deep in the wide water-holding cup.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in bright light for strongest color.

directions: An upright, urn-shaped rosette whose stiff leaves carry red-tipped spines and blush bright red toward the center. It takes more sun than most, which deepens the coloring, in a gritty, well-drained mix. Small white-and-lavender flowers appear in the flooded cup as the plant matures.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: This bold hybrid shows cream-and-green striped leaves overlaid with maroon banding and blotches. Bright indirect light keeps the variegation vivid without burning the foliage. Grow it in a shallow, open mix and keep the central cup topped with clean water.

planting.note: Mount or pot offsets in spring in bright, humid conditions.

directions: A tiny running species that spreads on stolons to form flask-shaped rosettes, ideal for mounting. The banded green-and-maroon leaves color best in bright indirect light with good air movement. Small blue flowers sit in the cup; let it clump into a colorful colony over time.

planting.note: Establish offsets in spring in bright, humid shade.

directions: This small stoloniferous species travels on wiry runners, its cross-banded leaves flushing pink and red in good light. Grow it mounted or in a shallow pan of open mix with steady humidity. It suits terrariums and mounted displays, forming spreading mats of little rosettes.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in bright light for richest color.

directions: A striking tubular hybrid whose broad leaves turn deep rose and coral in bright light. Grow it in a tall, well-drained pot and keep the vase filled with clean water. A pendant spike of pink bracts and blue-tipped flowers arches out at bloom, though it is grown mainly for foliage.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: This tubular species has dark, silver-banded leaves and is fairly forgiving of cool spells. Grow it in a compact, well-drained pot and let the mix dry a little between waterings. A drooping spike of rose-pink bracts and blue-violet flowers appears in the cooler months.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in a bright, airy spot.

directions: A slender clumping species that offsets freely into dense tubular colonies. Bright light and a fast-draining mix keep it compact; water into the cup and let the medium dry slightly. Nodding spikes of pink bracts and green-and-blue flowers appear in late winter to spring.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: A variable tubular species whose green leaves can flush and spot with red or bronze in good light. It clumps quickly and tolerates a range of conditions in a well-drained pot. The arching spike carries green flowers tipped blue among rosy bracts.

planting.note: Pot offsets in spring in bright, humid conditions.

directions: This handsome tubular species bears olive leaves flecked with pale spots and edged with fine teeth. Give it bright indirect light and a coarse, quick-draining medium, keeping the vase filled. A pendant inflorescence of pink bracts and green-blue flowers hangs from the maturing rosette.

planting.note: Establish offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: Named for the curled, recurved tips of its silver-spotted leaves, this compact tubular species is a collector favorite. Grow it bright and well-drained, letting the mix dry a little between waterings. A short nodding spike of pink bracts with blue-tipped flowers appears at bloom.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in a warm, humid, shaded spot.

directions: This terrestrial earth star hugs the ground with wavy leaves banded in bronze, green, and silvery cross-stripes. Grow it in a shallow pot of moist, well-drained mix with filtered light and high humidity. Small white flowers nestle in the center; offsets form between the leaves for propagation.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: Taller than most earth stars, this species and its variegated forms show green leaves striped cream, pink, and rose in bright light. Grow it in a moist but freely draining mix and avoid harsh direct sun. It spreads on short stolons, forming clusters of colorful rosettes.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in warm, humid shade.

directions: A large flat earth star with stiff, thick leaves boldly cross-banded in copper and gray, resembling pheasant feathers. Grow it in a shallow, well-drained container of moist mix under filtered light. Handle it gently, as the brittle leaves snap; propagate from the offsets it makes at the base.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in a warm, humid, shaded position.

directions: The original earth star, this small species forms flat green rosettes that can blush red in brighter light. Grow it low and moist in a shallow pot of open, well-drained mix with steady humidity. Fragrant white flowers appear in the center, and offsets crowd in around the parent.

planting.note: Plant offsets in spring in bright, filtered light for color.

directions: A popular small hybrid whose wavy leaves blush soft pink and rose over green in good light. Grow it in a shallow dish of moist, well-drained mix and keep humidity up around the foliage. Bright indirect light brings out the pink tones without scorching; propagate from basal offsets.

planting.note: Set out offsets in spring in bright, filtered light.

directions: This showy hybrid has cream-and-green striped leaves that flush vivid pink at the margins in bright light. Grow it in a shallow, well-drained pot kept lightly moist, with warmth and humidity. Strong indirect light intensifies the pink; increase it gradually to avoid burning the leaf edges.

planting.note: Mount or set out offsets once nights stay reliably above 50 F.

directions: Wire or glue the bulb-like base to bark, cork, or driftwood rather than potting it in soil, and never bury the swollen base. Soak the plant for twenty minutes weekly and shake out water trapped among the snaking leaves to prevent rot. A flush of red-tinted foliage and a slim violet spike signal that bloom is beginning.

planting.note: Position outdoors after the last frost when air stays warm and humid.

directions: Rest the hollow bulbous base on a bright shelf or mount it upright so its writhing gray leaves splay outward like snakes. Dunk it weekly, then invert it briefly so no water lingers in the cup. Expect a branched spike of tubular violet flowers rising from red bracts on mature specimens.

planting.note: One of the tougher species; set out in spring once hard frost has passed.

directions: Clump this stiff-leaved species onto bark or nestle it among rocks where it forms tidy rosettes over time. It tolerates cooler nights than most air plants but still resents frost and standing water. Bright pink bracts and deep blue-violet petals appear together in a short-lived spring display.

planting.note: Unlike most kin it accepts a loose bark-based potting mix; site after frost.

directions: This species grows happily in a fast-draining epiphyte mix, so set it in a small pot with the crown at the surface. Keep it evenly moist and give bright filtered light for the showiest color. A flat quill-shaped fan of rose-pink bracts emerges, opening a succession of deep purple flowers over many weeks.

planting.note: Site in warm, bright conditions after all danger of frost.

directions: Mount this curly species on cork or driftwood and keep it on the dry side, since heavy watering relaxes its signature ringlet leaves. When kept slightly thirsty the wide gray leaves curl tightly into spirals. A tall branched spike with pink bracts and purple flowers rises from the center at bloom.

planting.note: Move outdoors after frost into bright, airy shade.

directions: Set the open green rosette where it gets bright indirect light and good air movement, mounted or resting loose. Soak weekly and let it dry fully between waterings. As bloom nears the entire plant blushes brilliant red and short violet flowers open from the center.

planting.note: Place outside after frost in bright light to keep growth compact.

directions: This feathery, pine-needle-like species clusters into shaggy chains, so mount it where the offsets can ramble and cascade. Give strong light and regular misting or brief soaks. A single scarlet flower on a short stalk is a striking reward on each mature head.

planting.note: Prefers slightly more humidity; set out after frost in bright shade.

directions: Mount this small bulbous plant with its mottled, snakelike leaves upright so water drains from the swollen base. It appreciates a bit more humidity and slightly more frequent watering than desert types. A slender spike of purple flowers from pinkish bracts appears on mature plants.

planting.note: Set out in spring after frost; tolerates strong light.

directions: Grow this grassy species with its many thin, rush-like leaves upright in a bright spot where the fine foliage can arch. Soak weekly and shake dry, as water can collect at the leaf bases. The plant flushes reddish and pushes a small central spike when ready to flower.

planting.note: This high-Andes species wants strong light and very little water.

directions: Give this fuzzy, snow-white species the brightest light you can and water sparingly, since its dense trichomes harvest moisture from air and rot easily if soaked. A quick misting or short dip every couple of weeks is plenty. Pale pink bracts and white-to-lavender flowers eventually top the silvery mound.

planting.note: Site outdoors after frost in bright, airy conditions.

directions: This velvety, silver-gray rosette prefers bright light and drier care, so soak lightly and let it dry quickly. Its soft fuzz makes it prone to rot if water lingers in the crown. A slender arching spike of red bracts and violet-blue flowers is a dramatic finish.

planting.note: Set out after frost; keep the hollow base dry inside.

directions: Grow this large, hollow-based species upright so no water pools inside its ant-domicile cavity. Its silvery scaled leaves tolerate strong light and infrequent watering. A stout branched spike with rosy bracts and purple flowers rises from mature plants.

planting.note: Enjoys more humidity; site in bright shade after frost.

directions: This tiny, densely fuzzy species with a small swollen base likes higher humidity and regular light watering. Mount it upright and mist often rather than long-soaking. A short spike carries pink bracts and a striking purple flower far larger than the plant itself.

planting.note: Give bright light and steady humidity; set out after frost.

directions: Grow this delicate green species, with its needle-thin arching leaves, in bright filtered light and keep humidity up. Soak or mist regularly and never let it stay wet in the center. A branched spike of small violet flowers hovers above the airy foliage at bloom.

planting.note: Site outdoors after frost; airflow is essential for this fuzzy type.

directions: This silvery, wildly fuzzy rosette needs strong air movement and gentle watering because its dense hairs trap moisture. Dunk briefly, then let it dry fast in a breezy spot. Red bracts and violet flowers cap the plant when it finally blooms.

planting.note: A sturdy species; hang or perch it in bright light after frost.

directions: This large caulescent species anchors itself by curling its lower leaves around branches, so let it hang or hook it onto a perch. It handles strong light and infrequent watering well. A tall branched spike of lavender flowers carries a sweet grape-like fragrance.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light to bring up leaf color.

directions: Grow this upright rosette in bright light, where its slender leaves blush from green to deep red. Water weekly and empty any moisture caught in the tight center. Several fan-arranged spikes of red bracts and tubular purple flowers rise together at bloom.

planting.note: Set out after frost; tolerates heat and strong sun.

directions: This fine, silvery-leaved species clumps freely and tolerates hot, bright, dry conditions better than most. Mist or briefly soak and let it dry quickly. Bright yellow tubular flowers emerging from reddish bracts make it unusual among the largely purple-flowered genus.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light with good airflow.

directions: This tiny globe of hair-fine silver leaves radiating in all directions needs bright light and careful, sparing watering. A brief dip followed by fast drying keeps its fuzzy leaves from rotting. A slim red spike lifts a single bright violet flower well above the spiky ball.

planting.note: Enjoys humidity and gentle light; set out after frost.

directions: Grow this soft, silvery rosette of broad recurved leaves in bright indirect light with regular misting, as it favors humidity. Avoid harsh sun that can scorch its downy leaves. A branching spike opens delicate soft-pink flowers, uncommon in the genus.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright, airy shade.

directions: This open rosette with a velvety texture likes bright filtered light and weekly soaking. Its fine leaves flush deep red as flowering approaches, sometimes tipped in maroon. Short violet flowers open from the vividly colored center at peak bloom.

planting.note: Adaptable and vigorous; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: This robust, broad-leaved rosette is easygoing, taking bright light and weekly watering with a quick shake to empty the cup. Its wide leaves blush peachy red or copper as bloom nears. A dense central head of purple flowers emerges from the flushed rosette.

planting.note: A Florida and Caribbean native; site after frost in bright light.

directions: Mount this small bulbous-based species upright and keep it on the dry side, drying quickly after each watering. Its few gray leaves twist above a swollen pseudobulb. A pink-bracted spike with violet flowers rises from the pot-bellied base at bloom.

planting.note: One of the hardier, easiest species; set out after hard frost.

directions: This forgiving, fast-clumping species forms silvery mounds and tolerates cooler nights than most air plants. Soak weekly and give bright light for tidy growth. Ruffled blue-and-white flowers open over a long period, and it multiplies readily by offsets.

planting.note: A Texas and Mexico native; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: Mount this small bulbous-based native upright and keep water out of the hollow base to avoid rot. Its wiry, twisting leaves form clusters over time. A slim spike of rosy bracts and violet flowers appears on mature heads.

planting.note: A native Southeastern species; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: Grow this grassy native with its many thin, needle-like leaves mounted or clustered in bright light. It handles humid warmth and cooler nights and multiplies into dense tufts. Foliage often flushes red before a slender spike of small violet flowers appears.

planting.note: A Southeastern US native; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: This wiry, grass-like native forms tufts of narrow leaves and adapts well to humid Southern gardens on trees or mounts. Give bright light and let it dry between waterings. A red-bracted spike bears small violet flowers, and the plant reddens as bloom nears.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light; tolerates heat.

directions: This stiff, pointed rosette takes strong light and drier care, making it forgiving of neglect. Soak briefly and let it dry fully. A branched spike of red-and-yellow-green bracts carries violet flowers above the hard, silvery leaves.

planting.note: Fairly cold-tolerant; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: This small, wispy species with thread-like gray leaves clusters into loose tangles best displayed hanging. Give bright light and regular misting. Its bright yellow flowers are strongly and sweetly fragrant, an unusual trait in the genus.

planting.note: Set out after frost; keep the swollen base dry inside.

directions: Grow this silvery, bulbous species upright so no water collects in its hollow base. Its chalky white-scaled leaves handle strong light and infrequent watering. A stout spike with rosy bracts and purple flowers rises from mature plants.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light with good airflow.

directions: This tiny plant sends fine, silvery, thread-like leaves out from a small bulbous base, so mount it where the wispy foliage can splay. Water lightly and dry quickly. A slender red spike carries a bright violet-to-red flower far larger than the plant.

planting.note: Set out after frost in strong light; tolerates drought.

directions: This upright, caulescent species forms a tall stem of chalky white leaves and handles strong sun and dryness well. Water sparingly and let it dry fully. A long spike bears greenish-white to pale flowers, and it offsets along the stem.

planting.note: Fairly adaptable; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: Grow this silvery, symmetrical rosette in bright light with weekly watering. It forms a neat plant that offsets to make clumps over time. A branched spike of pink bracts and white flowers rises well above the foliage at bloom.

planting.note: Enjoys humidity and gentle light; set out after frost.

directions: This soft green rosette favors humidity and bright indirect light rather than harsh sun, so mist regularly. It clumps freely into full mounds. A rosy-pink branched spike carries many small bright-pink flowers in a showy display.

planting.note: Enjoys humidity; hang it in bright light after frost.

directions: This delicate, thread-leaved species forms trailing silvery tangles best displayed hanging. Give bright light and frequent misting since it likes humidity. Small sky-blue flowers appear in profusion and carry a light sweet scent.

planting.note: Adaptable and easy; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: This silvery rosette with recurving leaves clumps readily and adapts to a range of conditions. Soak weekly in bright light and shake out the center. A short spike of pink bracts opens pale white-to-pink flowers at bloom.

planting.note: Tolerates strong light and cool nights; set out after frost.

directions: This stiff, silvery-scaled rosette takes bright light and drier conditions well. Soak briefly and let it dry fully between waterings. A slender spike carries pale yellow flowers, an uncommon color in the genus.

planting.note: Easy and adaptable; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: This variable, small-leaved species forms dense clusters of stacked rosettes and is one of the easier air plants to grow. Give bright light and weekly water. White-to-blue flowers open above the tidy green or silver foliage.

planting.note: Forgiving and quick-clumping; set out after frost in bright light.

directions: This small silvery rosette multiplies quickly into rounded clumps and takes bright light with weekly watering. Rock-dwelling by nature, it does well mounted on stone or bark. Bright violet-blue flowers open from rosy bracts at bloom.

planting.note: Set out after frost in bright light; keep the base dry inside.

directions: Mount this tough species upright so water drains from its hollow base, and keep it in strong light. Its stiff, boldly striped and grooved leaves handle heat and drought. A thin spike of red bracts and violet flowers rises from mature plants.

planting.note: Set plants out in a sunny bog once hard frost has passed in spring.

directions: Grow in a peat-and-sand mix kept constantly wet with rainwater or distilled water, never tap water. The squat, rain-filled pitchers lie low and tolerate more cold than most Sarracenia, so leave plants outdoors through a dormant winter chill. Nodding maroon flowers open in spring before the new pitchers unfurl.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a wet, sunny site after the last hard freeze.

directions: Use a nutrient-free peat and sand medium standing in a shallow tray of pure water through the growing season. Tall, slender yellow-green pitchers rise in spring; give full sun to bring out subtle red veining. Provide a cool winter rest and pale nodding flowers will follow the next spring.

planting.note: Establish in spring in a sunny bog garden with acidic, peaty soil.

directions: Plant in pure peat and sand and keep the pot standing in mineral-free water. This rare mountain species makes tall green spring pitchers, then often switches to flat, non-carnivorous leaves in late summer. A firm cold winter dormancy is essential, so overwinter it outdoors or in an unheated space.

planting.note: Set out in spring in a permanently wet, sunny bog bed.

directions: Grow in live peat and sand kept soaking wet with rainwater. Slim, upright pitchers form a tidy clump, and the maroon spring flowers carry a distinct sweet fragrance. Allow a cold winter dormancy each year to keep the plant vigorous.

planting.note: Divide and replant rhizomes in early spring as growth resumes.

directions: This vigorous natural hybrid of the purple and yellow pitcher plants grows easily in wet peat and sand under full sun. Multiply it by cutting the creeping rhizome into pieces each with a growing point in spring. Its hardiness and reddish, sturdy pitchers make it one of the most forgiving Sarracenia for beginners.

planting.note: Divide the rhizome in early spring to propagate this named cultivar true to type.

directions: One of the most popular garden hybrids, it produces stout pitchers with heavily red-veined, ruffled lids over a coppery throat. Grow in wet peat and sand under strong sun and propagate only by division so the cultivar stays true. Give a cold winter rest and the red coloring intensifies in the following season.

planting.note: Divide rooted offsets in spring to keep this all-red cultivar true.

directions: Prized for leaves and traps that flush entirely deep burgundy, it needs bright direct sun to hold that color. Grow in pure peat and sand kept wet with distilled water, and resist triggering the traps for fun since each closure costs the plant energy. Give a cool winter dormancy and propagate by dividing the rhizome so the red form stays true.

planting.note: Separate offsets in spring; this large-trapped clone comes true only from division.

directions: This selection is famous for unusually large traps that can approach an inch and a half across on a well-grown plant. Give it full sun and a nutrient-free peat and sand mix standing in pure water. Let it go dormant in a cold winter, and increase your stock by dividing the clump rather than sowing seed.

planting.note: Divide offsets in spring to keep the short-toothed trait consistent.

directions: Instead of long marginal spines, this cultivar has short triangular teeth that give the trap a tidy, saw-edged look. Grow in wet peat and sand under bright sun using only rain or distilled water. Provide a cold winter rest each year and propagate by division so the distinctive tooth shape carries on.

planting.note: Surface-sow seed in spring on wet peat and do not cover it.

directions: The classic beginner sundew, it forms strap-shaped leaves coated in glistening, sticky dew that curls around trapped gnats. Scatter the fine seed on the surface of wet peat and sand, keep bright and humid, and it germinates within a few weeks. It self-sows freely and blooms pink within its first year from seed.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring; seed benefits from a cold, moist chilling first.

directions: This temperate sundew makes semi-erect, spoon-tipped leaves and thrives in permanently wet, sunny bog conditions. Sow the tiny seed on the surface of wet peat and give it a cold-moist period to break dormancy. Hardy forms produce a tight winter resting bud called a hibernaculum, so leave them cold and wet over winter.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed over winter, then surface-sow on wet peat in spring.

directions: This is the widespread northern sundew, a flat rosette of round, long-stalked leaves beaded with sticky red tentacles. Seed needs a cold-moist stratification, so sow it and expose the pot to winter cold before spring warmth triggers germination. Keep it in live sphagnum or wet peat under full sun, and let it form a hibernaculum for its winter dormancy.

planting.note: Chill seed over winter, then surface-sow in spring on constantly wet peat.

directions: This temperate species carries long, upright, spoon-ended leaves and favors cold, wet fens and bogs. Give the seed a cold stratification, then surface-sow on wet peat and keep it in bright light. Grow it in live sphagnum standing in pure water and allow a genuine cold dormancy each winter.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or propagate quickly from root cuttings.

directions: Unmistakable for its tall, Y-forked leaves that can branch several times, this Australasian sundew is vigorous and long-lived. Sow seed on the surface of wet peat and sand, or increase it fast from root or leaf cuttings. Keep it wet and sunny; temperate forms tolerate light frost and rest in cool winters.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or propagate more reliably from root cuttings.

directions: The largest sundew, it produces strap-like leaves up to two feet long that slowly coil around large prey. It prefers cool, bright, humid conditions and a well-drained peat and sand mix that stays moist rather than waterlogged. Since seed can be slow and fickle, root cuttings are the surest way to build up this South African rarity.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or divide the freely-forming root plantlets.

directions: This tropical Australian sundew makes a rosette of long, lance-shaped leaves and tolerates lower light than most of its relatives. Grow it warm and humid in wet peat, giving bright indirect light rather than harsh midday sun. It spreads readily from root plantlets, so a single plant soon fills its pot.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring on wet peat and keep in bright light.

directions: A tidy South African rosette sundew, it presents a flat wheel of wedge-shaped leaves tipped with sticky red tentacles. Surface-sow the seed on wet peat and sand and grow bright, where strong light turns the whole rosette rosy. It is easy, compact, and blooms lavender-pink, making it an excellent windowsill carnivore.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in warm, humid conditions.

directions: This forgiving highland species from the Philippines tolerates ordinary home temperatures better than most tropical pitcher plants. Grow it in an airy, peat-based epiphyte mix, water with pure water, and give bright, filtered light with high humidity. Waisted, hourglass pitchers hang from the leaf tips; propagate it easily from stem cuttings.

planting.note: Take stem cuttings in spring and root them warm and humid.

directions: A widely grown, adaptable species with green-and-red pitchers bearing two winged ridges down the front. Pot it in a loose peat, bark, and perlite mix, keep humidity high, and water only with rain or distilled water. It is one of the most beginner-tolerant Nepenthes and roots readily from cuttings.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring; this hybrid grows fast and forgivingly.

directions: This tough natural hybrid of ventricosa and alata is the pitcher plant most often sold in garden centers because it thrives as a houseplant. Give it bright, filtered light, a fast-draining peaty mix, and pure water, and it produces orange-red pitchers almost continuously. It is nearly indestructible and roots from cuttings within a couple of months.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in warm, very humid conditions.

directions: This lowland species makes showy, bell-shaped pitchers heavily blotched maroon on a pale ground with a flared, ribbed peristome. It needs steady warmth and very high humidity, so a terrarium or greenhouse suits it best. Grow in an open epiphytic mix, water with pure water only, and propagate from stem cuttings.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in spring in warm, humid conditions.

directions: An intermediate to highland species with elegant, elongated pitchers marbled in maroon and a distinctively toothed inner peristome. Provide bright, filtered light, high humidity, and cooler nights, and grow it in a loose, airy peat mix. It is a robust, popular parent of many hybrids and roots well from cuttings.

planting.note: Root basal or stem cuttings in spring in warm, humid conditions.

directions: Famous for producing some of the largest pitchers in the genus, this Philippine species has broad, blunt-tipped leaves and heavy, colorful traps. Give it bright, filtered light, warmth, and high humidity in an open, well-drained peat and bark mix. It grows slowly but steadily and is propagated from basal shoots or stem cuttings.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed over winter, then surface-sow on wet peat in spring.

directions: This temperate butterwort forms a flat rosette of greasy, sticky leaves that trap small insects on their upper surface. Seed needs cold stratification, so chill it moist over winter before spring germination on wet peat. It forms a tight resting bud for winter dormancy and lifts violet, spurred flowers in spring.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or propagate quickly from leaf pullings.

directions: A popular Mexican species with large pink flowers and rosettes that shift between sticky summer leaves and small, non-carnivorous succulent winter leaves. Grow it in a fast-draining mineral mix of sand, perlite, and a little peat, keeping it merely moist in winter. It roots easily from a single detached leaf laid on damp medium.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed over winter, then surface-sow in spring on wet peat.

directions: This European butterwort is prized for showy violet flowers that are large relative to its sticky green rosette. Give the seed a moist winter chill, then surface-sow in spring and keep the medium constantly wet. It is fully hardy, forming a compact winter hibernaculum and producing small bulb-like gemmae that make propagation easy.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or propagate from plantlets at the leaf tips.

directions: A southeastern U.S. butterwort with pale pink, primrose-like flowers, it stays actively growing rather than forming a dry dormant bud. Grow it in wet peat and sand kept constantly moist, in bright but not scorching light. It obligingly sprouts new plantlets at the tips of older leaves, so a clump multiplies on its own.

planting.note: Surface-sow in spring, or propagate from single leaf pullings.

directions: The largest Mexican butterwort, it can spread its sticky rosette to nearly a foot across and catches prey on both leaf surfaces. Grow it in an airy mineral mix and keep it moist in summer but nearly dry through its succulent winter phase. Detached leaves root readily, making it one of the easiest butterworts to multiply.

planting.note: Float divisions in still, acidic water in spring as growth restarts.

directions: This rootless aquatic plant drifts just under the surface, its feathery stems studded with tiny bladders that suck in water fleas and larvae. Grow it floating in a container of still, mineral-poor, slightly acidic water in full sun. Yellow snapdragon-like flowers rise above the surface in summer; propagate simply by dividing the floating mat.

planting.note: Divide and press clumps of the mat onto wet peat in spring.

directions: This charming terrestrial bladderwort blooms almost nonstop with tiny lavender-and-white flowers that resemble a rabbit's face. Grow it in constantly wet peat and sand in bright, filtered light, where it forms a dense living carpet. It multiplies fast, so propagate by simply lifting and replanting pieces of the mat.

planting.note: Divide and replant portions of the mat in spring on wet substrate.

directions: Grown both as a bog plant and as a popular aquarium carpeting species, it forms a low turf of fine, grassy leaves over prey-catching underground bladders. Keep it constantly wet or submerged in soft, acidic conditions with bright light. Small pale-violet flowers appear when grown emersed; increase it by dividing the spreading mat.

planting.note: Drop strands into shallow, still water in spring to establish.

directions: This small, tangled aquatic bladderwort grows as a mesh of thread-like stems near the water surface and is common in aquariums and bog trays. Give it shallow, still, mineral-poor water and strong light to trigger its tiny yellow flowers. It grows quickly into a floating mass, so thin and divide it to share or control it.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes or take leaf cuttings in spring to propagate.

directions: This unique Australian plant makes both flat, ordinary leaves and small, lidded ground-level pitchers with a ribbed, toothed rim. Grow it in a well-drained peat, sand, and perlite mix that stays moist but never stagnant, with bright light and cool nights. It grows slowly, and cool winter conditions with strong light bring out deep purple pitcher coloring.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed over winter, then surface-sow in spring.

directions: Named for its hooded, snake-like pitchers with a forked, tongue-like appendage, this West Coast native demands cool roots and pure water. Keep the soil constantly flushed with cold rain or distilled water, ideally shading or cooling the root zone in hot weather while the tops enjoy sun. Seed needs cold stratification, and plants also creep outward on stolons that can be separated.

planting.note: Sow in warm conditions after frost; a little smoke or heat treatment aids germination.

directions: This tropical Australian annual catches gnats on slender, dew-covered leaves that sparkle like a rainbow in sunlight. Sow seed on warm, wet peat and sand, since heat and sometimes smoke treatment improve germination, and grow it in bright, humid conditions. It flowers pale purple within a couple of months and completes its life cycle in a single warm season.

planting.note: Scarify seed and sow directly in the final pot, since it resents root disturbance.

directions: Unusual among carnivores, this Iberian dewy pine grows in dry, sunny, well-drained soil rather than a wet bog. Nick or lightly abrade the hard seed and sow it directly where it will grow, as the taproot hates transplanting. Water sparingly from below and give full sun; the sticky, downward-curling leaves smell faintly sweet and trap many insects.

planting.note: Set out only after all frost has passed and soil has warmed.

directions: Plant in a fast-draining cactus mix with the rosette sitting just above the soil line. Handle the leaves as little as possible, since the pale powdery farina rubs off permanently. Water only when the mix is fully dry and expect arching pink-orange bloom stalks in late spring on established plants.

planting.note: Move outdoors once nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Grow this compact species in a gritty, sharply drained mix and give it bright light to keep the maroon leaf mottling strong. Water sparingly, letting the pot dry completely between soakings. Slender reddish flower spikes rise in spring bearing small red and yellow bells.

planting.note: Transplant after frost when soil warms above 60F.

directions: Give this stiff, agave-like rosette full sun and a mineral-rich, fast-draining soil to bring out its red leaf tips. Water deeply but infrequently, allowing the root zone to dry out. Coral-and-yellow flowers appear on wiry arching stems in late spring and summer.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost date once soil is warm.

directions: Plant in a lean, gritty mix and grow in strong light, which deepens the dramatic dark reddish-black margins along each leaf. Water only when the soil is bone dry, and shelter from prolonged wet. Expect coral bell-shaped blooms on tall stalks in late spring.

planting.note: Move outside after frost has ended and nights warm.

directions: This hybrid is prized for the raised bumps that form near the leaf tips, which are most pronounced in bright light and cool nights. Use a fast-draining mix and water only when fully dry. Pinkish flowers appear on arching stems in late spring.

planting.note: Transplant outdoors after the danger of frost passes.

directions: Grow in full sun and gritty soil to hold the near-black leaf color, which fades to green in shade. Water only after the mix dries fully, avoiding water in the crown. Vivid scarlet flowers on dark stems appear from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: This large hybrid forms upturned, lilac-frosted leaves with a distinctive cupped shape. Provide bright light and a very well-drained mix, watering only when the soil is fully dry. Peach-colored blooms rise on tall arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Set outdoors after frost when nights stay warm.

directions: A large gibbiflora-type hybrid with ruffled, wavy-edged blue-green leaves that blush pink in strong sun. Grow in a deep, fast-draining pot and water only when dry. Tall flower stalks carry orange-red bells in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last frost date.

directions: This selection forms a dense rosette of blue-lavender leaves with crimped, ruffled pink margins. Give it bright light and gritty soil, watering only after the mix dries completely. Arching pink flower stems appear in summer.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when the soil is warm.

directions: This large species develops a broad rosette atop a thickening stem that lifts well above the soil with age. Grow in full sun and free-draining soil, and water only when dry. Tall branched inflorescences bear red-and-yellow flowers in autumn.

planting.note: Transplant after all frost has passed.

directions: This vigorous species forms powdery silver-blue rosettes and offsets freely to make tidy clumps. Plant in gritty soil with full sun, and water only when fully dry. Coral flowers appear on arching stalks in late summer and fall.

planting.note: Set outdoors after frost when nights warm.

directions: Named for the way its pale rosettes flush fiery red along the leaf edges under stress and strong sun. Grow in a fast-draining mix and water only after the soil dries. Orange-pink blooms rise on curved stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant out once frost danger has passed.

directions: This hybrid makes pointed, powder-blue rosettes that take on soft lavender-pink tones in cool bright conditions. Use gritty soil and full sun, watering only when the mix is dry. Yellow-orange flowers appear on slender stems in spring.

planting.note: Move outdoors after the last spring frost.

directions: Melaco forms glossy rosettes that shift from coppery green to rich caramel and mahogany in strong light. Grow in a sharply draining mix and water only after full dry-down. Orange bell flowers rise on arching stalks in spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil warms.

directions: This shrubby form has densely felted green leaves that blush deep red along the edges in sun and cold. Grow in bright light with gritty soil and water only when dry, keeping the fuzzy foliage itself dry. Yellow and red flowers appear on short stems in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when nights stay warm.

directions: This small species makes clusters of pale pink-and-blue rosettes that trail and spread readily from offsets. Grow in gritty soil with bright light to keep the color soft and pink, and water only when dry. Tiny white star flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Transplant after the last frost.

directions: This tight species forms flat green rosettes, each leaf ending in a fine hair-like bristle at the tip. Grow in mineral-rich, fast-draining soil with strong light, and water sparingly. Red-marked white star flowers open on short stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost; this species tolerates brief light chill.

directions: A small native species forming low gray-green rosettes flushed with pink or bronze in sun. Plant in very gritty soil and give it full light, watering only when fully dry. Star-shaped flowers with reddish centers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost date.

directions: This species spreads by thin stolons that root new silvery rosettes to form a low mat. Grow in gritty, well-drained soil with bright light and water only when dry. Small white flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Set outdoors after frost when soil warms.

directions: This vigorous intergeneric hybrid forms loose rosettes on trailing stems that turn coppery orange and pink in full sun. Grow in fast-draining soil and water only when dry to intensify the color. Yellow star flowers open in spring.

planting.note: Plant out after all frost has passed.

directions: Also sold as Bronze, this hybrid forms rosettes of pointed leaves that deepen to rich bronze-red in bright light. Grow in gritty soil, water only after full dry-down, and give as much sun as possible for color. Yellow flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost when nights stay warm.

directions: This hybrid makes powdery blue-gray rosettes that blush pink and lavender in sun, offsetting freely into clumps. Plant in fast-draining soil and water only when dry. Pinkish blooms rise on arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out after frost when soil warms.

directions: This species carries plump, powdery blue-pink leaves on sticky-stemmed rosettes that lengthen with age. Grow in bright light and very gritty soil, watering only when the mix is fully dry. Reddish bell flowers appear on arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost date.

directions: This selection bears large chunky leaves with a heavy blue-white bloom and pink margins in strong light. Use a sharply draining mix and water sparingly, handling leaves gently to preserve the farina. Bell-shaped flowers appear on curved stalks in spring.

planting.note: Transplant after frost when nights are warm.

directions: This species forms rosettes of rounded, silvery-blue leaves flushed pink or purple in sun. Grow in bright light and gritty soil, watering only when fully dry. Reddish flowers open on short arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out after all frost has passed.

directions: Unusual for the genus, this species has long, cylindrical green leaves tipped red in strong sun on sprawling stems. Grow in gritty soil with bright light and water only when dry. Red bell flowers appear on tall stalks in winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant out after frost when soil warms.

directions: This hybrid makes plump, powder-coated blue-white leaves that pick up pink tips in bright light. Grow in fast-draining soil and water only after full dry-down, avoiding the leaf surfaces. Bell-shaped flowers appear on arching stems in spring.

planting.note: Set out after the last frost date.

directions: This hybrid forms open rosettes of thick, frosted lavender-blue leaves that blush purple-pink under stress. Grow in gritty soil and bright light, watering only when fully dry. Pale bell flowers open on short stems in spring.

planting.note: Plant in spring; give afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: This variegated selection has gray-green leaves broadly edged in creamy white on upright stems. Grow in well-drained soil with full sun to light shade, watering moderately during establishment. Pale pink to white flower clusters open in late summer and fall.
